content:
I've discussed with Alice and Lyude before, but I think @Boqun Feng was left out of the loop until now.

Using CondVar with SpinLockIrq does not work, it results in a lot of lockdep splats instead.

The issue is that do_unlocked() releases the guard, but not the IrqDisabled token:

        // SAFETY: Switches to another thread. The timeout can be any number.
        let ret = guard.do_unlocked(|| unsafe { bindings::schedule_timeout(timeout_in_jiffies) });

This means that we go to sleep with IRQs still disabled.

Alice sketched out a solution for this, but she's on leave currently.
with the new SpinLockIrq semantics, this is not a problem because irq will be reenable at unlock time
Hi Boqun, how recent is that? I had a pretty recent version IIRC

How recent is your discussion? ;-) Lyude should be aware of the changes, the POC was posted like months ago:
https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20241018055125.2784186-1-boqun.feng@gmail.com/, and Andreas and Dirk had it tested.
I feel like I am waiting for a new version / non PoC. When that happens, I will add back the examples of hrtimer that use this feature.

Boqun Feng said:

How recent is your discussion? ;-) Lyude should be aware of the changes, the POC was posted like months ago:
https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20241018055125.2784186-1-boqun.feng@gmail.com/, and Andreas and Dirk had it tested.

I am using this https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20241018055125.2784186-5-boqun.feng@gmail.com/

Ok, so that's the old version, and my POC was posted as a reply to that version. What it does is allowing a refcounted interrupt disable handling, and so that SpinLockIrq can disable/enable interrupts itself, that makes the concept much simpler.
